# Page 1^page=1
B-Trees vs B+Trees And their impact on production database systems husseinnasser.com

# Page 2^page=2
Agenda ● Full Table Scans ● B-Tree ● B-Tree limitations ● B+Tree ● B+Tree Considerations ● B+Tree storage cost in MySQL vs Postgres ● Summary

# Page 3^page=3
Full Table Scan ● To find a row in a large table we perform full table scan ● Reading large tables is slow ● Requires many I/Os to read all pages ● We need a way to reduce the search space

# Page 7^page=7
B-Tree ● Balanced Data structure for fast traversal ● B-Tree has Nodes ● In B-Tree of “m” degree some nodes can have (m) child nodes ● Node has up to (m-1) elements

# Page 8^page=8
B-Tree ● Each element has a key and a value ● The value is usually data pointer to the row ● Data pointer can point to primary key or tuple ● Root Node, internal node and leaf nodes ● A node = disk page

# Page 16^page=16
Limitation B-Tree ● Elements in all nodes store both the key and the value ● Internal nodes take more space thus require more IO and can slow down traversal ● Range queries are slow because of random access (give me all values 1-5) ● B+Tree solves both these problems ● Hard to fit internal nodes in memory

# Page 17^page=17
ID , Name 1 , John 2 , Ali 3 , Rick 4 , Sara 5 , Edmond 6 , Ion 7 , Edmond 8 , Pete 9 , Oliver 10 , Yong 11 , Xui 4:704 TID 701 702 703 704 705 800 801 802 803 804 805 8:802 2:702 6:800 10:804 1:701 3:703 5:705 7:801 9:803 11:805 Find rows (ID between (4 and 9) in this b-Tree

# Page 18^page=18
B+Tree ● Exactly like B-Tree but only stores keys in internal nodes ● Values are only stored in leaf nodes ● Internal nodes are smaller since they only store keys and they can fit more elements ● Leaf nodes are “linked” so once you find a key you can find all values before and after that key. ● Great for range queries

# Page 19^page=19
5 B+Tree of Degree 3 8 3 2 4 1:701 6 2:702 3:703 4:704 5:705 6:800 7:801 8:802 9:803 10:804 11:805 7 9 10

# Page 20^page=20
5 Find rows (ID between (4 and 9) 8 3 2 4 1:701 6 2:702 3:703 4:704 5:705 6:800 7:801 8:802 9:803 10:804 11:805 7 9 10

# Page 21^page=21
B+Tree & DBMS Considerations ● Cost of leaf pointer (cheap) ● 1 Node fits a DBMS page (most DBMS) ● Can fit internal nodes easily in memory for fast traversal ● Leaf nodes can live in data files in the heap ● Most DBMS systems use B+Tree

# Page 22^page=22
5 B+Tree Storage cost 8 3 2 4 1:701 6 2:702 3:703 4:704 5:705 6:800 7:801 8:802 9:803 10:804 11:805 7 9 10 “Should” fit in memory Can remain in disk but preferred to be in memory

# Page 23^page=23
Storage Cost in Postgres vs MySQL ● B+Trees secondary index values can either point directly to the tuple (Postgres) or to the primary key (MySQL) ● If the Primary key data type is expensive this can cause bloat in all secondary indexes for databases such MySQL (innoDB) ● Leaf nodes in MySQL (InnoDB) contains the full row since its an IOT / clustered index

# Page 24^page=24
Summary ● Full Table Scans ● B-Tree ● B-Tree limitations ● B+Tree ● B+Tree Considerations ● B+Tree storage cost in MySQL vs Postgres
